clusters.title: Feeling angry makes people more likely to share news from low-credibility sources
clusters.summary: A new study reveals that feeling morally angry makes you more likely to share misinformation online. Researchers found that anger causes people to act impulsively and ignore source credibility, fueling the rapid spread of fake news.

items.title: Feeling angry makes people more likely to share news from low-credibility sources
items.description: A new study reveals that feeling morally angry makes you more likely to share misinformation online. Researchers found that anger causes people to act impulsively and ignore source credibility, fueling the rapid spread of fake news.

clusters.title: Political divide on climate policies is linked to a measurable gap in factual knowledge
clusters.summary: A recent psychology study suggests that political polarization over environmental policies is strongly linked to an underlying gap in objective, factual knowledge about the causes and consequences of climate change.

items.title: Political divide on climate policies is linked to a measurable gap in factual knowledge
items.description: A recent psychology study suggests that political polarization over environmental policies is strongly linked to an underlying gap in objective, factual knowledge about the causes and consequences of climate change.

clusters.title: Goodbye colonoscopy? New stool test detects 90% of colorectal cancers
clusters.summary: A breakthrough in microbiome research could change how colorectal cancer is detected—no colonoscopy required. Scientists used AI to map gut bacteria at an unprecedented level of detail, revealing subtle microbial patterns linked to cancer. By analyzing simple stool samples, their method identified 90% of cases, rivaling one of medicine’s most trusted diagnostic tools.

items.title: Goodbye colonoscopy? New stool test detects 90% of colorectal cancers
items.description: A breakthrough in microbiome research could change how colorectal cancer is detected—no colonoscopy required. Scientists used AI to map gut bacteria at an unprecedented level of detail, revealing subtle microbial patterns linked to cancer. By analyzing simple stool samples, their method identified 90% of cases, rivaling one of medicine’s most trusted diagnostic tools.

clusters.title: Depression may start with an energy problem in brain cells
clusters.summary: Researchers have discovered a surprising change in how cells produce energy in people with depression. Brain and blood cells in young adults with major depressive disorder produced more energy molecules at rest but had trouble increasing energy production when needed. Scientists believe this imbalance may contribute to symptoms such as fatigue and low motivation.

items.title: Depression may start with an energy problem in brain cells
items.description: Researchers have discovered a surprising change in how cells produce energy in people with depression. Brain and blood cells in young adults with major depressive disorder produced more energy molecules at rest but had trouble increasing energy production when needed. Scientists believe this imbalance may contribute to symptoms such as fatigue and low motivation. The finding could help pave the way for earlier diagnosis and more personalized treatments.

clusters.title: Scientists say 7 days of meditation can rewire your brain
clusters.summary: A single week of intensive meditation and mind-body practices led to measurable changes across the brain and body. Researchers observed improved brain efficiency, boosted immune signaling, and increased natural pain relief chemicals in participants’ blood. The effects even promoted neuron growth and stronger brain connectivity.

items.title: Scientists say 7 days of meditation can rewire your brain
items.description: A single week of intensive meditation and mind-body practices led to measurable changes across the brain and body. Researchers observed improved brain efficiency, boosted immune signaling, and increased natural pain relief chemicals in participants’ blood. The effects even promoted neuron growth and stronger brain connectivity. Surprisingly, the experience mirrored psychedelic-like brain states—without any drugs involved.

clusters.title: Microplastics in human liver could be fueling global surge in disease
clusters.summary: There is considerable evidence that microplastics and nanoplastics are present in the livers of humans, and wild animal populations on land and in the ocean. Now experts in environmental and human health are investigating whether the presence of these tiny plastic particles in the liver is driving disease and directly contributing to the soaring global rates of liver disease.

items.title: Microplastics in human liver could be fueling global surge in disease
items.description: There is considerable evidence that microplastics and nanoplastics are present in the livers of humans, and wild animal populations on land and in the ocean. Now experts in environmental and human health are investigating whether the presence of these tiny plastic particles in the liver is driving disease and directly contributing to the soaring global rates of liver disease.

clusters.title: For the first time, scientists pinpoint the brain cells behind depression
clusters.summary: Scientists have identified two specific types of brain cells that behave differently in people with depression, offering a clearer picture of what is happening inside the brain. By analyzing donated brain tissue with advanced genetic tools, the researchers found changes in neurons linked to mood and stress, as well as in immune-related microglia cells. These differences point to disruptions in key brain systems and reinforce that depression is rooted in biology, not just emotions.

items.title: For the first time, scientists pinpoint the brain cells behind depression
items.description: Scientists have identified two specific types of brain cells that behave differently in people with depression, offering a clearer picture of what is happening inside the brain. By analyzing donated brain tissue with advanced genetic tools, the researchers found changes in neurons linked to mood and stress, as well as in immune-related microglia cells. These differences point to disruptions in key brain systems and reinforce that depression is rooted in biology, not just emotions.

clusters.title: Food Stamp Work Rules Don’t Increase Employment, Researchers Say
clusters.summary: Work requirements will encourage people who are able to work to seek and maintain jobs, proponents say. But researchers haven’t found that they lower the unemployment rate.

items.title: Food Stamp Work Rules Don’t Increase Employment, Researchers Say
items.description: Work requirements will encourage people who are able to work to seek and maintain jobs, proponents say. But researchers haven’t found that they lower the unemployment rate.

clusters.title: These 80-year-olds have the memory of 50-year-olds. Scientists now know why
clusters.summary: A rare group of adults over 80, known as SuperAgers, are rewriting what we thought was possible for the aging brain. With memory abilities comparable to people decades younger, their brains either resist or withstand the damage typically linked to Alzheimer’s disease. Decades of research reveal that their social lifestyles and unique brain biology may hold the key to preserving cognitive function.

items.title: These 80-year-olds have the memory of 50-year-olds. Scientists now know why
items.description: A rare group of adults over 80, known as SuperAgers, are rewriting what we thought was possible for the aging brain. With memory abilities comparable to people decades younger, their brains either resist or withstand the damage typically linked to Alzheimer’s disease. Decades of research reveal that their social lifestyles and unique brain biology may hold the key to preserving cognitive function. Scientists believe these insights could pave the way for new strategies to delay or even prevent dementia.

clusters.title: Weight loss drug Ozempic cuts depression, anxiety, and addiction risk
clusters.summary: GLP-1 medications like semaglutide (Ozempic) may offer unexpected mental health benefits alongside weight loss. A large study found major drops in depression, anxiety, and psychiatric-related hospital visits among users. Even substance use disorders were significantly lower during treatment.

items.title: Weight loss drug Ozempic cuts depression, anxiety, and addiction risk
items.description: GLP-1 medications like semaglutide (Ozempic) may offer unexpected mental health benefits alongside weight loss. A large study found major drops in depression, anxiety, and psychiatric-related hospital visits among users. Even substance use disorders were significantly lower during treatment. Researchers suspect both lifestyle improvements and direct brain effects could be at play.

clusters.title: This common gout drug may slash heart attack and stroke risk
clusters.summary: A major new study reveals that treating gout may do far more than ease painful joint flare-ups—it could also protect the heart. Researchers found that patients who took common gout medications like allopurinol and successfully lowered their blood urate levels had a significantly reduced risk of heart attacks, strokes, and cardiovascular death over five years.

items.title: This common gout drug may slash heart attack and stroke risk
items.description: A major new study reveals that treating gout may do far more than ease painful joint flare-ups—it could also protect the heart. Researchers found that patients who took common gout medications like allopurinol and successfully lowered their blood urate levels had a significantly reduced risk of heart attacks, strokes, and cardiovascular death over five years.

clusters.title: Stroke triggers a hidden brain change that looks like rejuvenation
clusters.summary: After a stroke, the brain may do something surprisingly hopeful—it can “refresh” parts of itself. Researchers analyzing brain scans from over 500 stroke survivors found that while the damaged side of the brain appears to age faster, the opposite, unaffected side can actually look younger. This unexpected shift seems to reflect the brain’s effort to rewire itself, strengthening healthy regions to compensate for lost function.

items.title: Stroke triggers a hidden brain change that looks like rejuvenation

Researchers analyzing brain scans from over 500 stroke survivors found that while the damaged side of the brain appears to age faster, the opposite, unaffected side can actually look younger. This unexpected shift seems to reflect the brain’s effort to rewire itself, strengthening healthy regions to compensate for lost function.

clusters.title: A new study explores the boundary between everyday caffeine and panic
clusters.summary: A moderate dose of caffeine does not elevate baseline anxiety in people with panic disorder, though it may make them slightly more likely to step away from uncomfortable situations.

items.title: A new study explores the boundary between everyday caffeine and panic
items.description: A moderate dose of caffeine does not elevate baseline anxiety in people with panic disorder, though it may make them slightly more likely to step away from uncomfortable situations.

clusters.title: Fracture risk assessment becomes more accurate with age-based thresholds
clusters.summary: Fracture risk in Sweden has been overestimated, according to research from the University of Gothenburg. As health care tools are updated with new data, more people—including younger patients—may receive treatment. A widely used tool, FRAX, estimates the 10-year risk of osteoporotic fractures.

items.title: Fracture risk assessment becomes more accurate with age-based thresholds
items.description: Fracture risk in Sweden has been overestimated, according to research from the University of Gothenburg. As health care tools are updated with new data, more people—including younger patients—may receive treatment. A widely used tool, FRAX, estimates the 10-year risk of osteoporotic fractures. The Swedish model is still based on data from the 1990s, despite substantial changes in both fracture incidence and population demographics since then.

clusters.title: Hidden in hair follicles, immune 'sentinel' cells may help skin detect microbes
clusters.summary: Researchers at the School of Medicine at the University of California, Riverside have discovered previously unrecognized immune surveillance structures in the skin. Found within hair follicles, the cells resemble M (microfold) cells—specialized epithelial cells traditionally associated with the gut and airway tissues.

items.title: Hidden in hair follicles, immune 'sentinel' cells may help skin detect microbes
items.description: Researchers at the School of Medicine at the University of California, Riverside have discovered previously unrecognized immune surveillance structures in the skin. Found within hair follicles, the cells resemble M (microfold) cells—specialized epithelial cells traditionally associated with the gut and airway tissues.

clusters.title: Researchers complete 16-year analysis of pediatric substance use health care patterns
clusters.summary: Over the past 16 years, pediatric substance use-related visits (SUVs) to PEDSnet institutions have been increasing across all care settings and have increased at a greater percentage than visits overall, according to a comprehensive retrospective analysis. The largest proportion of SUVs among youth occur in the outpatient setting, and nicotine, cannabis and opioids are driving much of the growth.

items.title: Researchers complete 16-year analysis of pediatric substance use health care patterns

clusters.title: After assault, OCD risk rises fastest in first year, pointing to a critical care window
clusters.summary: Obsessive compulsive disorder (OCD) is a mental health condition characterized by recurring intrusive thoughts (i.e., obsessions) and repetitive behaviors (i.e., compulsions) aimed at reducing anxiety. This disorder is estimated to affect 1.2% to 2.3% of people every year.

items.title: After assault, OCD risk rises fastest in first year, pointing to a critical care window
items.description: Obsessive compulsive disorder (OCD) is a mental health condition characterized by recurring intrusive thoughts (i.e., obsessions) and repetitive behaviors (i.e., compulsions) aimed at reducing anxiety. This disorder is estimated to affect 1.2% to 2.3% of people every year.

clusters.title: Fish oil may be hurting your brain, new study finds
clusters.summary: Fish oil has long been praised as brain-boosting, but new research suggests the story may be more complicated. Scientists found that in people with repeated mild head injuries, a key omega-3 fatty acid in fish oil—EPA—may actually interfere with the brain’s ability to repair itself. Instead of helping recovery, it appears to weaken blood vessel stability, disrupt healing signals, and even contribute to harmful protein buildup linked to cognitive decline.

items.title: Fish oil may be hurting your brain, new study finds

clusters.title: Scientists found a protein that drives brain aging — and how to stop it
clusters.summary: Scientists have uncovered a powerful new clue in the mystery of brain aging: a single protein called FTL1. In aging mice, higher levels of this protein weakened connections between brain cells and led to memory decline. But when researchers reduced FTL1, something remarkable happened — the brain began to recover, rebuilding lost connections and restoring memory performance.

items.title: Scientists found a protein that drives brain aging — and how to stop it

clusters.title: How aggressive breast cancer turns off the immune system
clusters.summary: Researchers are launching a new project to crack the mystery of aggressive breast cancer, where predicting disease progression remains a major hurdle. By studying how tumors interact with and suppress the immune system, scientists aim to identify new biomarkers that reveal how the cancer evolves. Using real patient samples, the team hopes to turn earlier discoveries into practical clinical tools.

items.title: How aggressive breast cancer turns off the immune system
items.description: Researchers are launching a new project to crack the mystery of aggressive breast cancer, where predicting disease progression remains a major hurdle. By studying how tumors interact with and suppress the immune system, scientists aim to identify new biomarkers that reveal how the cancer evolves. Using real patient samples, the team hopes to turn earlier discoveries into practical clinical tools. The goal: more precise, personalized treatments that can outsmart even the most dangerous tumors.
